name: Cloak of Manannan
description: The Cloak of Manannan, also known as the Féth Fíada, is a legendary artifact from Irish mythology, belonging to Manannán mac Lir, the sea god. This magical cloak possesses the extraordinary ability to render its wearer invisible, providing unparalleled concealment and protection. It is not merely a garment of physical invisibility; it also grants the wearer the power to become unseen by magical means and even to subtly influence perception, making them effectively undetectable. The cloak is often described as being woven from the very mists of the sea or spun from spider silk imbued with potent enchantments. Its significance lies in its strategic advantage, allowing heroes and gods alike to infiltrate enemy strongholds, eavesdrop on crucial conversations, and escape perilous situations undetected. It is a symbol of cunning, stealth, and the unpredictable nature of the sea itself. The cloak appears in numerous tales, aiding figures like Cú Chulainn and other heroes in their quests. Its association with Manannán underscores its divine origin and potent magical properties, making it one of the most coveted and powerful items in Irish lore. The cloak represents the power of illusion and the ability to manipulate perception, essential qualities for navigating the often treacherous world of Celtic mythology. Its disappearance from recorded history only adds to its mystique, fueling speculation about its current whereabouts and the potential for its rediscovery.
subtitle: Veil of Invisibility and Illusion
currentLocation: Unknown, possibly lost to the sea or hidden in the Otherworld

keyMyths.title: Cú Chulainn's Use
keyMyths.description: Cú Chulainn is said to have utilized the Cloak of Manannan to infiltrate enemy camps and gather intelligence during his battles and quests. This highlights the cloak's strategic importance in warfare and espionage.

keyMyths.title: Manannán's Gift
keyMyths.description: The Cloak is considered a gift from Manannán, showcasing his generosity and strategic mind in aiding those he deems worthy. It represents divine intervention and support for mortal heroes.

sources.author: Lady Gregory
sources.description: Collection of Irish myths and legends
